steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: On the left: "an Elvis taxon is not the real thing" is an independent clause. On the right: "is a misidentified look-alike" is the predicate of a second independent clause whose subject is 'it.' The blank must supply 'however' (a conjunctive adverb) and 'it' (the subject of the second clause).

steps.title: Apply the rule for conjunctive adverbs between independent clauses
steps.content:
A conjunctive adverb joining two independent clauses requires either a semicolon before it and a comma after (IC; however, IC) or a period to end one sentence and begin another (IC. However, IC).

Choice D uses 'however. It': the comma already in the stem before the blank combines with 'however' so that 'however' acts as a parenthetical/terminal adverb modifying the first independent clause, and the period creates the sentence boundary. The new sentence then begins with the capitalized 'It,' properly separating the two independent clauses.

  • Choice A — 'however but it': redundantly pairs 'however' with the coordinating conjunction 'but,' and a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice B — 'however it': provides no punctuation or conjunction between the two independent clauses, creating a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — 'however, it': uses only a comma after 'however,' which is a comma splice — a conjunctive adverb requires a semicolon or period, not just a comma, to separate independent clauses. ✗
That matches choice D — D is correct.
